title: First-Time Home Buyer in BC: The Complete 2026 Guide
metaTitle: First-Time Home Buyer BC 2026 — Programs, Costs, Steps
metaDescription: Complete 2026 guide to buying your first home in British Columbia. FHSA, RRSP HBP, BC PTT exemption, 30-year amortization and step-by-step Greater Vancouver buyer's path.
excerpt: Five programs that genuinely matter, three that don't, and a step-by-step plan for buying your first home in Greater Vancouver this year.

body.heading: The programs that genuinely move the needle in 2026
paragraphs: There are five federal and provincial first-time buyer programs in 2026, and three of them are genuinely useful. The First Home Savings Account (FHSA) is the strongest. Up to $8,000 per year, $40,000 lifetime, deposit and withdrawal both tax-free when used for a qualifying first-home purchase. For couples, that's $80,000 of essentially free money compounding inside an investment account.

Second on the list: the RRSP Home Buyers' Plan (HBP). You can withdraw up to $60,000 per person from your RRSP toward a down payment, repaid over 15 years interest-free. Combined with the FHSA, a couple can build a $200,000 tax-advantaged down payment over a few disciplined years.

Third: BC's Property Transfer Tax first-time buyer exemption. Full exemption up to $500,000 of fair market value, partial up to $835,000. On a Burnaby townhouse at $720,000, this saves you about $11,400 in PTT — money that goes straight into your closing costs or down payment.

body.heading: What about the programs that get marketing love but don't help much?
paragraphs: The First-Time Home Buyer Incentive (FTHBI) — the federal shared-equity program — was wound down in 2024. The BC HOME Partnership shared-equity program ended in 2018. Both still get mentioned online; ignore them. The First-Time Home Buyer Tax Credit gives you a $1,500 tax credit on your federal return — useful, but small relative to your overall budget.

body.heading: 30-year amortization for first-time buyers — what changed
paragraphs: As of 2024, first-time buyers (and buyers of new builds, regardless of first-time status) can amortize an insured high-ratio mortgage over 30 years instead of 25. The federal stress test still applies, so this doesn't change the qualifying rate — but it does lower your stress-tested payment by roughly 8–12%, which directly raises your maximum mortgage.

On a $90,000/year income with no other debts, the difference between 25 and 30 years is roughly $40,000–$60,000 of additional buying power. Whether to actually take 30 years is a separate question — longer amortization means more total interest paid. Many of my clients qualify on 30 years but choose 25 once they see the lifetime numbers.

body.heading: Down payment minimums you'll actually face
paragraphs: Federally: 5% of the first $500,000 of purchase price plus 10% of the portion from $500,000 to $1,500,000. Above $1.5M, 20% on the entire amount. On a $950,000 Burnaby townhouse: 5% × $500,000 + 10% × $450,000 = $70,000 minimum.

Below 20% down, your mortgage must be insured. CMHC, Sagen and Canada Guaranty charge a one-time premium of 2.4–4.0% added to your mortgage. On a $880,000 mortgage with 5% down, that's about $35,000 added to your balance — usually rolled into payments rather than paid upfront.

body.heading: The actual step-by-step plan
bullets: Month -12: Pull your free Equifax report. Fix any errors. Open an FHSA if you don't have one.
Month -9: Maximize FHSA and RRSP HBP contributions. Pay down credit-card balances to under 30% of limits.
Month -6: Free pre-qualification call to see your realistic price range.
Month -3: Full pre-approval with rate hold. Now you can shop with realtors with confidence.
Month -1: Find the home, make the offer with a financing subject. We confirm appraisal and remove subjects within the agreed timeline.
Month 0: Closing day. Sign mortgage documents about a week before completion. Funds advance, title transfers, you get keys.
Year 5: 120 days before maturity, we re-shop your renewal — typically saves $5,000–$25,000.

callout.title: Want a free pre-qualification?
callout.text: Email me your situation in plain English. I'll send back a one-page realistic price range — no documents, no credit pull required.

faqs.question: Can I combine FHSA and RRSP Home Buyers' Plan?
faqs.answer: Yes — and you should. They stack. A disciplined couple maxing both can build a $200,000 tax-advantaged down payment in a few years.

faqs.question: What's the cheapest first-time buyer entry point in Greater Vancouver?
faqs.answer: Surrey Central, Coquitlam Town Centre, and parts of Burnaby Heights have condos starting around $500K–$650K in 2026. Townhouses run higher. East Vancouver entry-level condos are $650K–$800K.

faqs.question: Do I need 20% down?
faqs.answer: No — minimum is 5% on the first $500K and 10% from $500K to $1.5M. Above $1.5M you need 20%. The trade-off below 20%: CMHC insurance premium added to your mortgage balance.
